reminders: 3-min self-intro MAX → STOP and let him drive
BUSINESS-FIRST — avoid: multi-agent, LLM, FastAPI, autograd, LR from scratch
SAY: 2LoD | audit traceability | defensible | governance | conceptual soundness
AML IS risk management — don't apologize for no credit risk
ASK KING Q about his Wholesale → MV mindset shift

themes.label: Self-intro (3-min business-framed)
themes.focus: Open with 2LoD framing at Scotiabank. Lead with CRS validation work. Drop in 'defensible' and 'audit traceability'. End with Why MV in one sentence. 3 MIN HARD STOP.

themes.label: Why RBC / Why this role
themes.focus: Three reasons: (1) EMRM rigor + OSFI-facing, (2) methodology transfers from AML 2LoD, (3) RBC investing in AI Model Risk. Don't list beyond 3.

themes.label: Why Model Validation (not building)
themes.focus: Honest: discovered I liked validation parts of CRS (benchmarking, multi-model testing) more than pure building. Conscious preference.

themes.label: Most relevant project (S6 CRS, business-framed)
themes.focus: Frame as: behavioral risk scoring + multi-dimensional validation. Emphasize AUDITABLE, DEFENSIBLE, GOVERNANCE. Cut: DWT math, PySpark details, team politics.

themes.label: What do you know about MV?
themes.focus: Independent 2LoD review of 1LoD model package. Conceptual soundness, statistics, calibration, stability, documentation for OSFI. For RBC: AIRB PD/LGD/EAD.

themes.label: Credit risk experience / gap (honest bridge)
themes.focus: Not direct credit. But: methodological toolkit transfers (WoE/IV, PSI, conceptual soundness, regulatory doc). Reading Basel III / IFRS 9 / Wholesale vs Retail.

themes.label: Wholesale vs Retail bridge
themes.focus: Say this naturally ONCE during the meeting. My work = Retail-analog. Wholesale = LDP / Merton / expert judgment. Show curiosity about how team bridges them. Derek spent 6.5 yrs in Wholesale — he'll engage.

themes.label: Scotiabank background — AML as 2LoD
themes.focus: Frame AML as 2LoD risk management from the start. Transactional data, millions of rows, FINTRAC regulatory traceability, team of 3.

themes.label: Rotman MMA / education
themes.focus: Part-time while working = commitment to continued rigor. Covers risk finance + ML. Parallels Derek's own Schulich MBA path — natural bonding.

themes.label: FRM / designation question
themes.focus: If he asks / natural moment: 'I've looked at FRM — seems like a natural complement to MMA for a risk career.' Don't overclaim.

themes.label: Why leaving Scotiabank
themes.focus: Flexible end date in August. Looking for first full-time after graduation. Don't mention practicum or return offers.

themes.label: Strength
themes.focus: Combine statistical rigor + end-to-end execution. Examples: Validator layer in pipeline, challenged rolling-window on conceptual grounds.

themes.label: Weakness
themes.focus: Over-engineer / reach for complex model first. Fix: start simple, add complexity only when data justifies. Government + banking forced prioritization.

themes.label: Compensation
themes.focus: Don't anchor. 'Flexible, posted range for Senior Analyst works.' Let HR handle specifics.

themes.label: Other offers / timeline
themes.focus: Actively exploring, a few processes in progress. This role is strong fit. Flexible on timeline.

themes.label: Career goals 3-5 yrs
themes.focus: Near-term: deepen credit risk validation + ML model validation. Longer-term: help shape validation methodology for AI/ML models. RBC is where that's happening.

themes.label: Anything else to know
themes.focus: Short, confident close. Genuine excitement, ready to ramp up on credit risk, can contribute day one on statistical/validation toolkit.

themes.label: KING Q — ask Derek about his shift
themes.focus: WORD-FOR-WORD memorized. Ask after he describes the role (~35 min mark). Invites him to share, proves research, shows 1LoD/2LoD awareness.

themes.label: Other Qs to ask Derek
themes.focus: Pick 1-2 more beyond the King Q. Skip compensation / WFH / team size.

themes.label: Thank-you email template
themes.focus: Send within 24h. Reference ONE specific thing he said. Short. Confident. No technical flex.

themes.label: EMERGENCY — tech grilling
themes.focus: If Derek unexpectedly goes deep: be honest about limits, pivot to methodology, don't fake. Have LR / KS talking points ready.

themes.label: EMERGENCY — dead air
themes.focus: If conversation stalls: offer to walk through a specific validation example from CRS. Have 2-min business-framed version loaded.

intro_3min.title: 3-Minute Self-Intro (business-first framing)
bullets: [OPEN — 15s] 'Thanks for making time, Derek. I'll keep it under three minutes.'
[CURRENT ROLE — 45s] I'm currently on the AI and Machine Learning team at Scotiabank, supporting the AML business line — so a 2nd Line of Defense context.
My day-to-day is customer risk scoring, anomaly detection, and feature engineering on millions of transactions across the FINTRAC reporting channels.
Everything we build has to be defensible to compliance and to regulators — so I've developed a natural muscle for documentation, statistical validation, and audit traceability.
[SIGNATURE PROJECT — 45s] My most relevant project has been building a Customer Risk Score framework — a 0 to 100 behavioral risk scoring model on transactional data.

A core part of my work was the validation layer: I benchmarked feature-engineering approaches side-by-side, tested signals across both supervised and unsupervised models, and scaled validation from Pandas prototype to PySpark at production volume.
That methodology work is what drew me toward Model Validation as a focus.

[EARLIER + EDUCATION — 30s] Before Scotiabank — and still ongoing part-time — I was at Canadian Heritage, where I built a classification model that automated 98% of a manual review process, saving roughly $700K annually.
That project taught me to defend model choices to non-technical policy makers — which I now see as core to validation work.
I did my undergrad in Statistics and Computer Science at U of T, and I'm finishing my MMA at Rotman this year.

[WHY MV — 15s] What's drawn me to this role specifically is that the validation lens — rigorous, regulatory, adversarial — maps directly onto the work I've been gravitating toward anyway. Happy to go deeper on any of that.
⚠️ STOP AT 3 MIN — let him drive next
⚠️ DO NOT MENTION: multi-agent pipeline, LLM, FastAPI, React, self-correction loop

intro_why_rbc.title: Why RBC / Why this role
bullets: Three reasons.

First — RBC's EMRM is one of the most methodologically rigorous validation groups in Canadian banking. Independent 2LoD, OSFI-facing, covering AIRB parameters, acquisition, and account management models. That's the level of rigor I want to develop in.

Second — my Scotiabank work is already in a 2LoD risk context. AML, FINTRAC compliance, customer risk scoring with KS and IV. The methodology transfers directly; the domain shift is from AML risk to credit risk.

Third — the JD explicitly mentions AI/ML techniques and deep learning, and I noticed RBC has a dedicated Director, AI Model Risk. That's one of the few validation teams actively evolving methodology for modern ML models — which is where our field is heading.
⚠️ STOP — three reasons only, don't pile on

intro_why_mv.title: Why Model Validation (not building)
bullets: Honestly — I discovered I liked the validation parts of my projects more than pure model building.

In my CRS project at Scotiabank, my favorite part wasn't training models — it was challenging a teammate's rolling-window proposal on conceptual grounds, benchmarking alternatives side-by-side, and testing features across multiple models to see if signal held.
In my regulatory pipeline work, the most interesting design problem was: what makes an auto-generated feature trustworthy? KS? IV? Schema checks? Audit logging?
Model validation is where that adversarial, rigorous mindset lives full-time. And in credit risk it has the highest stakes — these models drive regulatory capital and affect real customer outcomes.
⚠️ Frame as conscious preference, NOT apology for 'not wanting to build'

S6_business.title: Most Relevant Project — S6 CRS (business-framed)
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, our team of three built a Customer Risk Score framework — a 0-to-100 behavioral risk scoring model on transactional data. Think of it as a behavioral risk scoring model analogous in methodology to a PD-style model, just in an AML context.
[MY VALIDATION WORK] A core part of my work was the validation layer across three dimensions.

First — conceptual soundness. My teammates initially proposed rolling-window features: 30 / 60 / 90 day counts. I challenged that design because rolling windows produce multiple rows per customer, which breaks customer-level granularity. I proposed an alternative that preserves one-row-per-customer and benchmarked the two side-by-side on sample data to show the evidence.

Second — multi-model testing. We validated features across both supervised and unsupervised models. The principle was: if the signal is real, it should show up regardless of the model we fit. Signal that only appears in one model is suspicious.
Third — scale validation. We prototyped in Pandas, then reran the entire pipeline in PySpark at production volume — millions of rows across 6 FINTRAC channels — and verified the results held.
[RESULT] The feature set passed multi-model validation at production scale. The CRS framework is now the foundation for the team's next phase.
⭐ WORDS TO USE: validation layer, conceptual soundness, benchmarking, multi-model testing, scale validation, defensible, auditable
⚠️ DO NOT SAY: DWT, Discrete Wavelet Transform, rolling window math details, team politics
⚠️ STOP — ~90 seconds total

S5_business.title: Backup — S5 Pipeline (if he asks for something else)
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, I built an automated system that turns regulatory text into validated detection features — reducing days of manual feature engineering to minutes.

[VALIDATION-CENTRIC FRAMING] The key design constraint was that nothing could go live without rigorous validation. In a regulated environment, you can't put generated features in front of compliance decisions if you can't defend them.

[THE VALIDATION LAYER] I designed a multi-tier validation framework: code safety checks, schema alignment checks, and statistical validation using KS statistic and Information Value. If a feature falls below the IV threshold, the system auto-iterates — every attempt logged for audit trail.
Every feature also traces back to the original regulatory text — full auditability, the same discipline OSFI would expect.
[RESULT] The pipeline achieved strong performance while maintaining full regulatory traceability — a property that's non-negotiable in a regulated setting.
⚠️ DO NOT SAY: 'multi-agent', 'LLM', 'FastAPI', 'self-correction loop' — use 'automated system', 'validation framework'
⚠️ Keep to ~60 seconds if backup

mv_overview.title: What do you know about Model Validation?
bullets: At a high level — independent 2nd Line of Defense review of 1LoD's model package.
Checking conceptual soundness — does the model form fit the data?
Statistical performance — does it discriminate well? AUC, KS, Gini.
Calibration — does it get the level right, not just the ranking?
Stability — does it hold up over time and across segments? PSI, back-testing.
And documentation — every finding packaged for internal governance and OSFI.
For RBC specifically — AIRB parameters: PD, LGD, EAD — feeding into regulatory capital. EL equals PD times LGD times EAD.

My direct experience is on the Retail-analog side through AML. I'm actively learning the Wholesale structural modeling approach — LDP, expert judgment, migration matrices — because it's quite different.
⭐ KEYWORDS: conceptual soundness, discrimination, calibration, stability, documentation, AIRB, EL formula

credit_gap.title: Credit Risk Gap (honest bridge)
bullets: I'll be transparent — I haven't validated AIRB parameters directly or worked on Basel-regulated credit models.

What I have done is the methodological foundation: statistical model validation using KS, IV, PSI; regulatory compliance modeling under FINTRAC; customer risk scoring on transactional data; large-scale feature engineering in a 2LoD environment; and model-form conceptual soundness assessment.
I've started reading the credit risk domain — PD, LGD, EAD estimation, Basel III capital framework, IFRS 9 ECL — and my MMA at Rotman covers risk finance.
The gap is domain terminology, not methodology. I'd ramp up quickly because the statistical discipline is the same.
⚠️ Confidence tone — not apology
⚠️ Have one credit-risk term ready if he probes: 'Basel III three-pillar framework' or 'AIRB requires PD, F-IRB only PD'

wholesale_retail.title: Wholesale vs Retail Bridge (say this once, naturally)
bullets: I know EMRM covers both Retail and Wholesale portfolios.
My direct experience has been on Retail-analog work at Scotiabank — transactional and behavioral scoring.
I've been reading about how Wholesale is quite different — Low Default Portfolios, Merton-style structural models, heavier expert judgment, benchmarking against Moody's and S&P ratings.
I'm curious how the team bridges those two methodologies in practice — whether that's shared tooling, separate sub-teams, or something else.
⭐ WHY THIS LANDS WITH DEREK: He spent 6.5 years in Wholesale Credit Risk before moving to EMRM. This signals you've researched him and understand the portfolio split.
⭐ Expected reaction: he'll engage, possibly tell you about the team structure.

scotia_background.title: Scotiabank Background — AML as 2LoD
bullets: I'm on the AI and Machine Learning team at Scotiabank, reporting into the AML business line.
AML is a 2nd Line of Defense function — we sit between the front-line business units and the external regulators at FINTRAC.
Our work: customer risk scoring, anomaly detection, feature engineering on millions of transactions across 6 FINTRAC reporting channels — EFT, large cash, casino, and others.
What's shaped my mindset: every output has to be defensible to compliance and to regulators. Traceability, documentation, statistical validation aren't nice-to-haves — they're table stakes.
Team of 3 data scientists, my senior manager Phume published the research paper that informed some of our ML work.
⭐ KEYWORDS: 2LoD, FINTRAC, defensible, traceability, millions of transactions

mma_background.title: Rotman MMA / Education
bullets: I'm finishing my Master of Management Analytics at Rotman this year — part-time while working.
Core coursework: machine learning, statistical modeling, risk finance, data engineering. A lot of it has reinforced the statistical toolkit I use at Scotiabank.
Undergrad was Honours BSc in Statistics and Computer Science at U of T — so the quantitative foundation was set there.
The combination — applied statistics + CS + business analytics + current banking experience — feels like the right stack for model validation.

⭐ IF he opens up: Derek did Schulich MBA 2017-2019 part-time while working. Natural bonding point — mention 'working through a program while being in the role really forces you to integrate the theory into what you're doing day-to-day'.

frm_question.title: FRM / Designation Question
bullets: If he asks about certifications, or the topic of credentials comes up:
'I've earned my Azure DP-100 already, and I've been looking at FRM — it seems like a natural complement to the MMA for a risk-focused career.'
'I'd welcome your perspective on timing — given you've gone through CFA, CPA, CFP, and Schulich MBA, you've probably thought a lot about how to sequence this kind of thing.'
⭐ Shows you've thought about your trajectory
⭐ Mentions FRM — the risk-specific designation, relevant to this role
⭐ Asks him for mentor-level advice (flattering but genuine)
⚠️ Don't overclaim — if you haven't actually looked into FRM, say 'I'm considering FRM' instead

leaving_scotia.title: Why Leaving Scotiabank
bullets: My current role has a flexible end date in August.
I'm looking for my first full-time role after graduation, and I'm specifically targeting places where I can grow into a long-term career.
⚠️ STOP — don't mention 'practicum', don't mention 'return offers', don't apologize

strength.title: Strength
bullets: My strength is combining statistical rigor with end-to-end execution.

On the rigor side — I designed a validation layer using KS and IV thresholds. I challenged a teammate's feature proposal on conceptual grounds and benchmarked the alternative. I rejected logistic regression at Canadian Heritage on conceptual-soundness grounds before I even built the model.
On the execution side — I've shipped end-to-end at every role: SQL extraction to Power BI dashboards at Canadian Heritage, PySpark pipelines to anomaly detection at Scotiabank.
The combination — rigorous analytical thinking plus the ability to actually deliver — is what I'd bring to validation work.
⚠️ STOP

weakness.title: Weakness
bullets: Earlier in my career I tended to over-engineer — reaching for complex models when a simpler approach would answer the business question fine.
I've gotten better by forcing myself to start with the simplest viable solution first, and only add complexity when the data justifies it.
Working in government and banking environments helped — non-technical stakeholders force you to prioritize traceability, explainability, and business impact over elegance.
⚠️ STOP — don't pile on examples

comp.title: Compensation
bullets: I'm flexible on compensation. The posted range for Senior Analyst / GG08 in GRM works for me. Happy to let HR handle the specifics when we get there.
⚠️ DO NOT anchor a specific number in this meeting
⚠️ If he pushes: 'I'd expect it to be in line with market for Senior Analyst at a Big 5 bank in Toronto.'

offers.title: Other Offers / Timeline
bullets: I'm actively exploring opportunities and have a few processes in progress.
This role is a strong fit for what I'm looking for long-term. I'm flexible on timeline.
⚠️ STOP — don't overshare. Don't mention TD rejection. Create mild scarcity without lying.

goals.title: Career Goals (3-5 yrs)
bullets: Near-term — deepen expertise in credit risk model validation, especially at the intersection of traditional PD/LGD/EAD methods and modern ML techniques. That's where the methodology is evolving fastest.
Longer-term — take on more technical depth and eventually help shape how validation methodology evolves for next-generation AI/ML models used in credit decisioning.
RBC's EMRM feels like the right environment for that trajectory — especially with a dedicated AI Model Risk leader in the team.
⚠️ DO NOT SAY 'manager' — Derek wants people who want to grow technically, not just climb

anything_else.title: Anything Else You'd Like Me to Know?
bullets: Just that I'm genuinely excited about this role.
The combination of rigorous validation methodology, regulated-environment experience, and RBC's scale — it's exactly the depth I want to build my career on.
I'm ready to ramp up on credit risk domain knowledge quickly and contribute on day one with the statistical and validation toolkit I already use daily.
⚠️ STOP — short, confident, warm close

ask_derek_king.title: KING Q — Ask Derek (memorize word-for-word)
bullets: Ask this AFTER he describes the role (usually around the 30-35 min mark).
'Before we wrap, I'd love to ask you something specific.'
'I noticed you spent 6+ years in Wholesale Credit before moving into EMRM about two years ago.'
'What's been the biggest mindset shift for you — from doing the credit analysis to validating the same types of models?'
⭐ Proves research. Invites him to share. Reveals his 1LoD/2LoD perspective. Opens lots of follow-up.
⭐ Follow-up if he answers warmly: 'Is that shift something you see new analysts wrestle with too?'
⭐ Or: 'Do you still miss the credit analysis side, or has validation become the more interesting work for you?'
⚠️ Do NOT deliver mechanically — pause, make eye contact, let it land

ask_derek_other.title: Other Qs to Ask Derek (pick 1-2 beyond King Q)
bullets: Team dynamic: 'How does EMRM balance the perspectives of quant-heavy directors and business-focused leaders like yourself? I imagine the challenge is getting both lenses on every validation.'

AI model validation: 'How is EMRM thinking about ML and AI model validation as the industry evolves? I noticed RBC has a dedicated AI Model Risk director — does that team and yours share methodology?'
FRM / designation (mentor framing): 'You hold CFA, CPA, and CFP. For someone at my stage finishing MMA, would you see FRM as a natural next designation, or is there another path you'd recommend?'
Growth: 'What distinguishes a great Senior Analyst on this team from a solid one? What do you notice in the first year that predicts who grows fastest?'
⚠️ DO NOT ask: compensation, benefits, WFH, team size, 'typical day'

thankyou_email.title: Thank-You Email (send within 24h)
bullets: Subject: Thank you — Meet & Greet
Hi Derek, Thank you again for the conversation this afternoon. I appreciated [ONE SPECIFIC THING HE SAID].

Our conversation reinforced my interest in the Senior Analyst role. The combination of rigorous 2LoD methodology, the breadth from Retail through Wholesale, and a team investing in ML model risk is exactly the environment I'm looking to grow in.
Please let me know if there's anything else helpful from my side as the process moves forward.
Best regards, Jerry (Zongheng) Li

⭐ Fill-in examples: 'your perspective on the shift from Wholesale Credit into validation mindset', 'the way EMRM is scaling ML model validation', 'your advice on the FRM designation', 'hearing how the team balances business and quant perspectives'
⚠️ KEEP IT SHORT — 4-5 sentences max
⚠️ Send from zongheng.li@rotman.utoronto.ca

tech_emergency.title: EMERGENCY — if tech grilling unexpectedly
bullets: Derek's LinkedIn suggests he's unlikely to go deep tech — but if he does: be honest about limits, pivot to methodology, never fake.

If asked 'explain logistic regression': 'Models log-odds as a linear function of features. Sigmoid maps that to a probability. Trained by maximizing log-likelihood, equivalent to minimizing cross-entropy. In credit, the output is interpreted as a PD, and coefficients translate to odds ratios via exponentiation — business-intuitive reading of each feature's effect.'

If asked 'what's KS statistic': 'Maximum vertical distance between the cumulative distributions of goods and bads across the score range. In credit, benchmarks are typically 0.4 to 0.6 — higher suggests overfitting or leakage.'

If asked 'what's PSI / drift': 'Population Stability Index — how much a distribution has drifted from baseline. Sum over bins of (actual - expected) times log of the ratio. Under 0.1 stable, 0.1-0.25 monitor, above 0.25 investigate. Common pitfall: fix bin edges from baseline, don't re-bin independently.'
If asked something you don't know: 'I haven't worked with that directly. The principle I'd apply is [X]. Could you walk me through how the team thinks about it?'
⚠️ Never bluff. Derek has 14 years reading candidates.

dead_air.title: EMERGENCY — dead air / conversation stalls
bullets: If silence or the conversation loses energy after ~20 min:
Option 1 — Offer to share: 'Would it be helpful if I walked through a specific validation example from my CRS project? I think it illustrates the mindset I'd bring.'
Option 2 — Ask a good question early: move King Q up if the middle is dragging.
Option 3 — Pivot to his team: 'I'd love to hear more about how you structure the team day-to-day. Are analysts typically assigned to a specific portfolio — Retail vs Wholesale — or do you rotate?'
⚠️ If HE seems unengaged / short: don't panic, don't oversell, listen actively, ask good questions, let him talk. Sometimes Directors just have bad days.
⭐ You control your side. Do your job well. Rest is noise.

my_questions.title: QUESTIONS FOR DEREK — ask the King Q + 1-2 others
items: [KING] You spent 6+ years in Wholesale Credit before moving to EMRM about two years ago. What's been the biggest mindset shift for you — from doing credit analysis to validating the same types of models?
How does EMRM balance quant-heavy directors with business-focused leaders like yourself? I imagine the challenge is getting both lenses on every validation.
How is EMRM thinking about ML and AI model validation as the industry evolves? I noticed RBC has a dedicated AI Model Risk director.
You hold CFA, CPA, and CFP — for someone at my stage finishing MMA, would you see FRM as a natural next designation?
What distinguishes a great Senior Analyst on this team from a solid one? What predicts who grows fastest?
